1:
The witnesses open the chapter by dwelling on a silence — the long interval God allowed before he spoke. The grammatical-historical commentators fix its length and purpose. Keil and Delitzsch note that the Lord left David almost a whole year in his sin, sending no prophet until the adulterous child had been born (2 Sam. 11:27; 12:15), so that the fruit of the crime should first be brought to light and the hardened sinner deprived of any power to deny or conceal it; God would break the unbroken heart by the torture of its own conscience before charging it by the mouth of Nathan, and they point to Ps. 32 as David's own record of that year's misery. Ellicott adds the personal detail that Nathan was already on intimate terms with the king, recognised by him as a prophet since the great promise of 2 Sam. 7.

The puritan Matthew Henry measures the same nine months pastorally: David's comforts were suspended and his communion with God interrupted, so that in all that time he penned no psalms — his harp was out of tune, and his soul 'like a tree in winter, that has life in the root only.' Henry then marvels at the messenger. When the thing displeased the Lord, one would have thought he would send enemies, terrors, and the messengers of death; instead he sent a prophet, David's faithful friend, to instruct and counsel him. The reformed Gill agrees that David lay long without any true sense of his sin, his graces dormant and the joys of salvation gone, while the enemies of religion blasphemed; Nathan is sent 'to awaken and arouse him,' the fruit of which was the public repentance of Ps. 51.

The wesleyan voices stress the divine initiative and the prophet's art. Wesley observes that when the ordinary means had failed to awaken David, God took an extraordinary course, so that the merciful God pities and prevents the man who had so horribly forsaken him; and Nathan prudently ushers in his reproof with a parable 'after the manner of the eastern nations,' that he might surprise David and cause him unawares to give sentence against himself. Clarke says the same — the prophet frames a curious parable by which David is led to pronounce his own condemnation. The one shade of difference among them is how black to paint the silent months: Henry 'willingly hopes' David lamented in secret before God, while Keil reads the anguish of Ps. 32 as the self-inflicted torment of a heart still concealing its guilt. All agree on the governing note that Henry states: though God may suffer his people to fall into sin, he will not suffer them to lie still in it.
2:
The parable's opening picture of the rich man's abundance draws the witnesses into its double sense — a scene realistic enough to disarm David, yet aimed straight at his sin. The grammatical-historical commentators keep both edges in view. Ellicott explains the whole design: the parable is meant to rouse David's indignation against the offender without being so transparent as to awaken, at first, any suspicion of a personal application; it does not allude to the special crimes of adultery and murder, but to the meanness and selfishness of the transaction — qualities David was still in a condition to appreciate. He sets it beside the like use of parables by the wise woman of Tekoah (2 Sam. 14:2-11) and the prophet who trapped Ahab (1 Kings 20:35-41). Keil and Delitzsch simply carry the narrative forward, letting the rich man's 'exceeding many flocks and herds' stand as the measure of a wealth that made his one act of plunder inexcusable.

The reformed Gill and the wesleyan Wesley agree on the allegory's key. The many flocks and herds, in which the wealth of men lay in those times and countries, signify David's many wives and concubines — the six he had in Hebron and the more he took in Jerusalem, besides his master's house that had been given him. Wesley compresses it to a phrase: the many flocks note 'David's many wives and concubines.' The point the interpreters press is the same one Nathan is building toward: a man so richly supplied had not the poor man's excuse of need, and every additional possession only deepened the guilt of coveting the one thing that was not his.

There is no dispute among the witnesses here, only a shared admiration for the prophet's wisdom. The verse teaches, they hold, how reproof may be made to disarm before it wounds. By clothing the charge in a story of flocks and herds, Nathan secures David's judgement before David suspects himself to be the defendant; the very self-satisfaction that had dulled the king's conscience is enlisted to convict him. What looks like a complaint brought by a subject against an oppressor is in truth the net that v.7 will draw tight, and the abundance sketched in this verse is the first thread of it — for the sin Nathan exposes is not want but wantonness, the covetousness of a man to whom God had already given more than enough.
3:
The poor man's one little ewe lamb is the tender centre of the parable, and the witnesses linger over its pathos and its meaning. The reformed Gill reads the allegory plainly: the poor man had nothing but one little ewe lamb — Uriah had but one wife, much younger than he, and therefore called a lamb, an ewe lamb, a little one. He passes on a Jewish tradition from Abarbinel that Uriah had been a widower with children, older than Bathsheba, though he offers it only as a supposition of the parable. The wesleyan Wesley touches the single word 'bought,' glossing it 'as men then used to buy their wives,' or simply 'had procured' — a homely note on the customs behind the figure.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott admires the artistry. Quoting the Speaker's Commentary, he observes that 'all these circumstances are exquisitely contrived to heighten the pity of the hearer for the oppressed, and his indignation against the oppressor.' The lamb that grew up with the poor man's children, ate of his own meat, drank of his own cup, and lay in his bosom, is drawn so as to make its seizure feel like a wound to a household. The one caution comes from the wesleyan Clarke, who notes that the lamb lying in the man's bosom 'can only mean' that it was a pet or favourite in the family; taken otherwise the circumstance would be unnatural, and he candidly calls it 'the only part of this parable which is at variance with nature and fact' — a domesticated lamb so cherished being an unusual thing.

Beneath the details the witnesses hear one steady note: the preciousness of what the rich man took. The lamb was 'unto him as a daughter,' and the whole force of the parable turns on that intimacy, for it answers to the tie between Uriah and the one wife who was to him as his own soul. Where Gill and Wesley decode the figure into Uriah and Bathsheba, Ellicott and Clarke attend to the storyteller's craft that makes the decoding land with such weight; but all agree that Nathan has painted love, not merely property, so that David's coming anger in v.5 will be the anger of a man who feels the outrage before he sees himself in it. The lamb's tenderness is the hinge on which the king's self-condemnation will turn.
4:
This verse turns from the poor man's tenderness to the rich man's act, and with only one witness speaking at length the synthesis draws its whole substance from the figure of the traveller who comes and must be fed. The reformed Gill reports the two readings the Jewish and Christian expositors had given to that wayfaring man. Some, he says, understand him of Satan, who came to David and stirred up his lust by the temptations that offered — the adversary who is 'a walker,' as the word used signifies, going about seeking whom he may devour, and who is with good men only as a wayfaring man that does not abide, whose temptations, when they succeed, are as meat and drink to him. But the Jews generally, Gill notes, understood the traveller of the evil imagination or concupiscence in man — the lustful appetite in David that 'wandered after another man's wife, and wanted to be satiated with her.'

That second reading opens onto a piece of moral psychology the witnesses elsewhere draw out (following Bishop Patrick and the Jewish writers): the evil disposition is called a traveller because in the beginning it is only that — a passing thing; but in time it becomes a guest, and in conclusion is master of the house. The man who is a mere 'traveller' at the opening of the verse is a 'man,' a husband, by its close. So the figure carries its own warning about the growth of an indulged desire, which comes as a stranger and stays as a lord.

What gives the picture its sting is the rich man's refusal to spend his own. He 'spared to take of his own flock and of his own herd' and seized the poor man's lamb instead — an ingratitude and a cruelty that mirror David's own, who had wives in abundance yet reached for Uriah's one. The lesson the verse presses is the meanness of sin that will not be satisfied with what is lawful and its own: covetousness pampers itself at another's cost, and the traveller who might have been turned away at the door is entertained with stolen fare. Nathan has now set the whole scene, and David, feeling only the injustice of the tale, is ready to burst out in the sentence of v.5 — a judgement that will fall, unawares, upon his own head.
5:
David's anger blazes out, and the witnesses read his verdict as at once the mark of a conscience not wholly dead and the snare that will catch him. The grammatical-historical Ellicott sees in the flash of temper a proof of the man beneath the sin: David's generous impulses had not been extinguished, nor his warm sense of justice; his naturally quick temper — the same that had flared in the affair of Nabal (1 Sam. 25:13, 22, 33) — at once roused his indignation to the utmost. The oath, 'As the LORD liveth, the man that hath done this thing shall surely die,' shows a heart still capable of hating cruelty, even while blind to its own.

The wesleyan voices weigh the justice of that sentence and find it excessive. Clarke parses the Hebrew, which literally calls the offender 'a son of death,' a very bad man deserving to die; yet he observes that the law did not sentence a sheep-stealer to death but to fourfold restitution (Exod. 22:1), which is why David in the next breath demands the lamb restored fourfold. Wesley presses the irony harder: the doom is 'more than the fact deserved, or than he had commission to inflict.' It is observable, he says, that David, 'now when he was most indulgent to himself, and to his own sin, was most severe and even unjust to others' — a severity of temper that reappears in the harsh treatment of the Ammonites in v.31, both belonging to the time of his impenitence.

The witnesses thus hold two truths together without contradiction. On the one hand, the quickness to condemn wrong testifies that David was 'not essentially a bad man,' as Ellicott will say at v.7 — his sense of right survived his fall. On the other, the very heat of the sentence betrays a soul out of joint with itself: a man will often be hardest on the sin he is most guilty of, because judging another is cheaper than facing his own case. The reformed Gill notes only that David spoke 'taking it for a real fact,' which is precisely Nathan's design — to secure a true verdict from an honest instinct before turning it, in the words 'Thou art the man,' against the judge himself.
6:
The sentence of fourfold restitution sets the witnesses to weighing the number, and here a small textual and legal debate opens. The reformed Gill records that the fourfold repayment is 'according to the law' of Exod. 22:1, yet notes an alternative: because the Hebrew word is of the dual number, Kimchi thought it signified double the four, so that the sentence was to restore eight lambs, the rich man being bound to more because he had robbed the poor; and he passes on Weemse's view that the Hebrews double in the dual number until they reach seven. The grammatical-historical Ellicott, however, holds firmly to the plain four. It is, he says, 'in exact accordance with the Law' (comp. Exod. 22:1; Luke 19:8), and he treats the larger numbers of the ancient versions — the Septuagint's 'sevenfold' (comp. Prov. 6:31) and the Chaldee's 'fortyfold' — as expressions of 'more of human indignation,' insisting that David knew the Law too well to change its terms.

Keil and Delitzsch, joining the grammatical-historical reading, look past the arithmetic to the point Nathan is about to make. The parable was so selected that David could not suspect its reference to himself; therefore the words that follow, 'Thou art the man,' came upon him with all the greater shock. As in the parable the sin is traced to its root — insatiable covetousness, the man who 'had no pity' — so the divine indictment will trace David's crime to the same hidden background, that the greatness of his iniquity and the condemnation it deserved might be brought thoroughly home to his heart.

The difference among the witnesses is therefore narrow and friendly: whether 'fourfold' is to be taken at its face (Ellicott, following the Law) or heightened by a grammatical nicety to eightfold (Kimchi, reported by Gill), and how much weight to give the versions that swell it further. On the substance they are one. David has, out of his own mouth and by the measure of God's own statute, decreed both restitution and death against the offender — and Clarke will observe at v.7 how literally the fourfold penalty returned upon David's own house in the deaths of four of his sons. The verse is the trap sprung shut: the king has passed just sentence, and the sentence is his own.
7:
With the three words 'Thou art the man,' Nathan turns the whole parable on the king, and the witnesses gather around the shock and the mercies it recalls. The grammatical-historical Ellicott makes the psychology central: the boldness and suddenness of the application bring a shock that at once aroused David's slumbering conscience — and, he insists, this could not have happened had David been essentially a bad man. He was a man 'whose main purpose in life was to do God's will,' but who had yielded to temptation, been entangled in deeper guilt through the effort to conceal his sin, and had his conscience 'stupefied by the delirium of prosperity and power.' Now the deed is suddenly set before him in its true light. Ellicott ranges the moment beside the other great prophetic rebukes of kings — Samuel to Saul (1 Sam. 15:22-23), Elijah to Ahab (1 Kings 21:21-24), Nathan's own successors down to Matt. 14:3-5.

The reformed and puritan witnesses stress the recital of grace that Nathan sets against the crime. Speaking now, as Wesley says, 'not as a petitioner for a poor man, but as an ambassador from the great God,' the prophet opens with 'Thus saith the LORD God of Israel,' a name sacred and venerable to David. Gill and Henry unfold the reminder: God had anointed David king, preserved him from Saul's hand, given him his master's house and the house of Israel and Judah, and would have added more — 'See how liberal God is in his gifts,' Henry says; 'where he has given much, yet he gives more,' so that God's bounty is a great aggravation of the discontent that covets forbidden fruit.

The wesleyan Clarke fastens on the terror of the word itself and its aftermath. 'What a terrible word!' — by it David 'appears to have been transfixed, and brought into the dust.' He then traces, with something like awe, how the fourfold restitution David had decreed was exacted of his own house: four sons cut off by untimely death — the child of Bathsheba, Amnon, Absalom, and Adonijah — while the sword and calamity never departed from his family. 'Behold the goodness and severity of God!' The witnesses do not divide. Together they hold that the arrow found the joint in the armour: a conscience honest enough to condemn a stranger is made, by three words, to condemn itself, and the tenderness of the divine reminder makes the ingratitude of the sin unbearable.
8:
Nathan's catalogue of gifts — 'thy master's house, and thy master's wives into thy bosom' — draws the witnesses into both the history behind it and the aggravation it presses. The reformed Gill takes the 'master's house' not of Saul's palace at Gibeah but of his family, wealth, and above all his kingdom, all forfeited through the rebellion of Ishbosheth and succeeded to by David. Keil and Delitzsch, with the grammatical-historical school, hold the plain thrust of the whole recital: 'I gave thee thy master's house... and if that had been too little, I would moreover have given unto thee such and such things,' so that the sin of v.9 is set against a background of overflowing divine generosity.

The phrase 'thy master's wives' provokes the chapter's sharpest point of historical difficulty, and here Clarke and Ellicott do the careful work. The wesleyan Clarke suspects the words may mean no more than that God had given David 'absolute power over every thing possessed by Saul,' the possession of the royal harem being, by eastern custom, the sure proof of succession to regal right — as Absalom would later publish his usurpation by lying with his father's wives. Ellicott sharpens the problem: Scripture names only one wife of Saul (1 Sam. 14:50) and one concubine (2 Sam. 3:7), the latter taken by Abner; if Saul had others, David could not have received them until more than seven years after Saul's death. Both therefore read the clause as invoking the Oriental custom by which the new king inherited the harem of his predecessor, rather than asserting a literal cohabitation.

The interpreters agree that the force of the verse is moral, not merely historical. Ellicott states it directly: in these two verses the prophet enumerates the chief favours shown to David 'so as to show not only his base ingratitude, but also the unreasonableness of this particular sin.' A man to whom God had given kingdom, deliverance, and every royal right had no want that could tempt him — his crime was pure wantonness. The lesson the witnesses draw is the one already sounded over the parable: the greatness of the giver measures the greatness of the ingratitude, and the more God has heaped upon a man, the more monstrous is his reaching after what God forbade. What David lacked was nothing; what he despised, as the next verse will say, was the commandment of the Lord.
9:
The heart of the indictment falls in this verse — 'Wherefore hast thou despised the commandment of the LORD?' — and the witnesses trace both the sin despised and the crime it produced. The reformed Gill identifies the commandment precisely: it is the law of God, 'particularly the sixth and seventh precepts,' the prohibitions of murder and adultery, which David had shown no regard unto but slighted and despised by breaking them. The wesleyan Wesley enlarges the object of contempt from the Hebrew: David despised not only the commandment in general but 'the particular word of promise' God had already sent him by Nathan, that he would build him a house (2 Sam. 7); had he valued that sacred promise he would not have polluted his house with lust and blood. To marry the woman he had defiled and whose husband he had slain was, Wesley adds, 'an affront upon the ordinance of marriage,' making it not only to palliate but in a manner to consecrate such villainies.

The grammatical-historical and wesleyan voices together fasten on the two verbs the verse uses for Uriah's death. Clarke drives the guilt home: 'Thou hast killed Uriah — Thou art the Murderer, as having planned his death; the sword of the Ammonites was Thy instrument only.' Ellicott sharpens the same point grammatically: the second word, rendered 'hast slain him,' is stronger than the first 'killed,' and 'might well be translated murdered'; it was murder in the eyes of the Lord, although accomplished indirectly by the sword of the Ammonites. The aggravation, Keil elsewhere notes, lies in that very indirection — that the deed was done by the sword of the uncircumcised enemies of God's people.

What unites the interpreters is the theology of sin the verse exposes: that its essence is contempt of God. As Gill and Henry both press, the malignity of David's fall was its making light of the divine law and lawgiver, as if the obligation were weak and the threats not formidable — 'though no man ever wrote more honourably of the law of God than David did,' he is here justly charged with despising it. The verse refuses David every shelter: he cannot plead ignorance of the command, for he had honoured it in words; he cannot disown the murder, for planning a death is committing it; and he cannot consecrate the marriage that crowned the crime. The sin is named for what it is, and the sentence of v.10 follows as its just wages.
10:
The first stroke of the sentence — 'the sword shall never depart from thine house' — sets the witnesses to weighing how far that 'never' reaches. The grammatical-historical commentators read it as a bounded, not an endless, term. Keil and Delitzsch insist the word 'for ever' must be held in its literal force, yet argue that 'thy house' here does not mean David's line continued through his descendants but his house as existing under David himself, until broken up by his death; the threat began in the murder of Amnon (2 Sam. 13:29), continued in the death of Absalom the rebel (18:14), and was consummated in the execution of Adonijah (1 Kings 2:24-25). Ellicott agrees: the words 'for ever' and 'never' are constantly used to express the longest time possible in connection with the subject in hand, so here the sense is 'as long as David lives,' and the punishment found its realisation 'in a long succession of woes, from the murder of Amnon to the execution of Adonijah.'

The reformed Gill reads the reach more widely. The sword did not depart during David's life, as the slaughter of his sons showed, but also stretched beyond it — into his posterity, 'through their wars with the children of Israel, and other nations.' Where Keil and Ellicott close the threat at David's grave, Gill lets it run on into the troubled history of the dynasty; the difference is one of scope, both sides grounding the woe in the same catalogue of family bloodshed.

Beneath the question of duration the witnesses agree on the moral logic Nathan states: 'because thou hast despised me.' Here, as Henry observes, is a hard truth about the covenant of grace: God had promised that his mercy should not depart from David's house (2 Sam. 7:15), yet now threatens that the sword should not depart. Can mercy and the sword stand together? They can — for those may lie under great and long afflictions who are yet not excluded from the grace of the covenant. The pardon of v.13 will not cancel the chastisement of v.10; forgiveness lifts the guilt while leaving the rod, so that God may at once love his child and hate the sin, and make one sin the punishment of another within the sinner's own walls.
11:
The second stroke answers the second sin: 'I will raise up evil against thee out of thine own house... and give thy wives unto thy neighbour.' The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch draw out the exact correspondence of crime and penalty. David's twofold sin was to be followed by a twofold punishment: for his murder he must witness murder done in his own family, and for his adultery the violation of his wives, both in an intensified form — done not in secret but before all Israel and in the face of the sun. As his sin began in adultery and was consummated in murder, so 'the law of just retribution' would run its course; and the prediction was literally fulfilled when Absalom, at Ahithophel's counsel, went in to his father's concubines on the palace roof (2 Sam. 16:21-22).

The wesleyan voices are exercised by a harder question the verse raises: how God can be said to 'give' David's wives to another for so foul a deed. Clarke answers by the doctrine of permissive providence: 'In the course of my providence I will permit all this to be done.' Had David been faithful, God by his providence would have turned all this aside; but now, by his sin, 'he has made that providence his enemy which before was his friend.' Wesley reads the clauses in the same key — the evil comes 'from thy own children and family'; 'before thine eyes' means David shall know it as certainly as if he saw it, yet be unable to hinder it; and God 'shall by my providence give him power over them.' Wesley adds a shrewd note on the prophecy's deliberate obscurity: God expresses it darkly — naming a 'neighbour' rather than a son — 'that the accomplishment of it might not be hindered' by too plain a warning.

The witnesses converge on the terrible fitness of the sentence. The sin was secret and industriously concealed; the punishment should be open, and industriously proclaimed, so that, as Henry puts it, 'as face answers to face in a glass,' the penalty answers to the crime — blood for blood and uncleanness for uncleanness. God does not merely repay; he repays in kind and in public, that all Israel might read in David's shame how much he hates sin even in his own people, and that he will not let it go unpunished wherever he finds it. The very providence that had crowned David is now turned, by David's own hand, into the instrument of his chastening.
12:
The contrast the verse sets — 'thou didst it secretly, but I will do this thing before all Israel, and before the sun' — is read by the witnesses as the seal upon the retribution and the threshold of David's repentance. The reformed Gill dwells on the secrecy David had labored to keep: he committed his adultery privately and then 'endeavoured to conceal it, by getting her husband killed in battle, and then marrying her as soon as he could to hide the shame of it.' The concealment is the very thing God will overturn, exposing to the sun what David buried in the dark.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch mark this as the moment of breaking. 'These words went to David's heart, and removed the ban of hardening which pressed upon it.' He confessed to the prophet, 'I have sinned against the Lord' — words, Keil notes with the Berleburg Bible, 'very few, just as in the case of the publican' (Luke 18:13), and that fewness is itself 'a good sign of a thoroughly broken spirit.' There is no excuse, no cloaking, no palliation of the sin; no searching for a loophole, no pretext, no human weakness pleaded — he acknowledges his guilt openly, candidly, and without prevarication. In response to that candid confession Nathan announced the forgiveness of v.13.

The two schools do not divide; they read the verse from its two ends and meet in the middle. Gill looks back at the sin the sunlight will expose, Keil forward to the heart the sentence will break. Together they set out the moral the passage teaches: that sin's instinct is concealment and God's way is disclosure, and that the mercy of the exposure is precisely what shatters the hardened conscience. The public sentence pronounced here is not vindictiveness but the last blow of a chastening love, and its immediate fruit is the shortest and truest of confessions. What David had hidden for nearly a year is now to be spread before all Israel; and the man who had schemed to keep his sin out of sight is brought, in the next verse, to lay it bare before the Lord.
13:
Here fall the great words of confession and absolution — 'I have sinned against the LORD... The LORD also hath put away thy sin; thou shalt not die' — and the witnesses divide over just how much the pardon grants. The grammatical-historical Ellicott measures the confession against a false parallel. The same words 'I have sinned' were used by Saul (1 Sam. 15:24, 30), but 'in a totally different spirit': Saul's was a concession to the prophet to secure his support, with no real penitence, while David 'pours out before God the confession of a broken heart.' On 'thou shalt not die' he notes that David had committed two capital crimes — adultery (Lev. 20:10) and murder (Lev. 24:17) — but as an absolute monarch had no earthly authority to fear; the divine word matters to him 'far more as an assurance of forgiveness than as a warding off of any possible earthly danger,' and stands parallel to 'the Lord hath put away thy sin.'

The wesleyan Clarke resists any idea that full pardon is here pronounced. David 'as an adulterer was condemned to death by the law of God,' and Nathan is authorized only to assure him he shall not die 'a temporal death,' being preserved alive that he may have time to repent and find mercy. Clarke presses a striking argument: if the fifty-first Psalm was written after this interview, then David 'had not received pardon for his sin from God at the time he composed it,' for in it he still confesses his crime to find mercy (Ps. 51). And he reads the Hebrew of the absolution as 'the LORD hath caused thy sin to pass over,' or transferred it — the legal punishment laid upon the child, who shall die that David may live. Wesley states the narrower sense plainly: the sin is put away 'so far as concerns thy own life,' David not dying as by his own sentence in v.5 he had deserved.

The reformed Gill reads the mercy more fully. The confession, 'though short,' was 'a full one,' arising from thorough conviction, real brokenness of heart, and a godly sorrow that Ps. 51 records; and the 'thou shalt not die' that Henry expounds means not only sparing of the body but that David 'shall not die eternally, nor be for ever put away from God.' So the debate is real: whether Nathan announces a stay of temporal execution with pardon still to be sought (Clarke, Wesley), or the settled forgiveness of the penitent whose guilt is cancelled and who 'shall not come into condemnation' (Gill, Henry). All agree on the sweetness of the mercy — that God is ready to forgive the moment the sinner truly turns — and that the reprieve of the guilty father is bought, as the next verse shows, at the cost of the guiltless child.
14:
The pardon is granted, yet a penalty remains — 'because by this deed thou hast given great occasion to the enemies of the LORD to blaspheme, the child also that is born unto thee shall surely die' — and the witnesses labor to show why the forgiven sin must still bear so public a fruit. The reformed Gill names the deed in its full weight: 'this complicated wickedness, adultery with Bathsheba, and the murder of her husband, and occasioning the death of others.' It was sin that had furnished the irreligious of that generation with matter to triumph over David's fall and to speak ill of God and his law — the great evil, as Henry says, in the scandalous sins of those who profess religion, that they arm the enemies of God with occasion for reproach and blasphemy (Rom. 2:24).

The grammatical-historical Ellicott states the principle most fully. Although David was forgiven, since his sin had brought great scandal on the church 'it was necessary that he should suffer publicly the consequences of that sin' — important in David's case both for the vindication of God's justice and 'to destroy the hope that other sins also might go unpunished.' Yet he sets that special judgment within a wider and sobering law: the far greater part of David's sufferings flowed from 'the natural consequences' of his sin, consequences which the immutable moral government of the world brings home 'however the sinner may have repented and been forgiven.' The death of a little infant in a great king's harem might seem a light stroke, but David saw it in its true light — as evidence of God's unalterable purpose and a sign of the greater judgments to come.

Keil and Delitzsch add that the sentence was inflicted without delay: when Nathan had gone home, the Lord smote the child, which the next verse records (v.15). The witnesses do not divide. They hold together the two truths the verse binds — that pardon is real and free, and that sin, even pardoned, leaves consequences that the honour of God requires to be seen. The child's death is not the payment of David's debt, which grace has cancelled, but the public vindication of a holy God before a watching and blaspheming world, and the first token that mercy to the soul does not exempt the sinner from the rod upon his life.
15:
Nathan withdraws, the Lord strikes the child, and the witnesses read the scene both for the prophet's dignity and for the depth of David's grief. The reformed Gill notes that Nathan departed 'having delivered his message, and brought David to a sense of his sin,' with nothing more from the Lord to say. The puritan Matthew Henry, who treats this whole passage as a single movement, adds that Nathan went home 'probably to pray for David, to whom he had been preaching' — and observes with delight that God so honoured this faithful prophet that David named a son by Bathsheba Nathan (1 Chron. 3:5), and it was 'that son of whom Christ, the great prophet, lineally descended' (Luke 3:31).

On the child's sickness Henry finds a doctrine as well as a distress. That the Lord struck it, so that it was very sick, is one of those 'sensible proofs of the original sin in which they are conceived' — the sufferings and death of infants who have not sinned after the similitude of Adam's transgression. But the weight of his exposition falls on David's humiliation, which he reads as 'an evidence of the truth of his repentance.' In fasting and lying all night upon the earth David showed himself willing to bear the shame of his sin and to have it ever before him; he showed a tender, compassionate spirit rare in men of war; he showed a concern for another world, interceding while the child was yet within the reach of prayer; and he showed a holy dread of God's displeasure, deprecating the death chiefly as the token of that displeasure — 'Lord, chasten me not in thy hot displeasure' (Ps. 6:1).

Henry adds that when Nathan had retired David himself retired, and it was then, in all probability, that he penned the fifty-first Psalm — for though assured his sin was pardoned, he still prays earnestly for pardon and laments his fall, since 'then will true penitents be ashamed of what they have done when God is pacified towards them' (Ezek. 16:63). The witnesses do not contend; they see in the stricken child at once a proof that God will vindicate his honour, as v.14 had warned, and the occasion of a repentance whose reality is measured by David's sackcloth on the ground. The stroke that fell to answer sin becomes the furnace in which a broken and contrite heart is proved.
16:
David's fasting intercession for the dying child raises the question the witnesses most want to answer: how could he pray at all against a doom so plainly declared? The wesleyan voices resolve it by the conditionality of the threat. Wesley says David besought God 'supposing the threatening might be conditional, and so the execution of it prevented by prayer,' and went into his closet. Clarke feels the difficulty more keenly — 'How could he do so, after the solemn assurance that he had from God that the child should die?' — and answers that the justice of God required the penalty be exacted, so that the sentence 'could not be reversed'; yet, as he will explain at v.22, David and all under the Mosaic dispensation were so persuaded that God's threatenings were conditional that even here he sought a change.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott guards David's motive from two misreadings at once. The prayer implies neither 'any want of submissiveness to God's will' nor 'an inordinate love for the child of his guilt.' Quoting O. von Gerlach, he finds that in a man whose penitence was so deep the prayer must have sprung from another source than excessive love of any creature: David's great desire was to avert the stroke as a sign of the wrath of God, hoping that in the child's preservation he might discern a proof of divine favour consequent upon his restored fellowship — and that when the child died he humbled himself and rested satisfied with grace, 'without giving himself up to fruitless pain.' Yet Ellicott will not let the natural affection be wholly erased: 'David's deep love for the child is not to be overlooked altogether.'

Keil and Delitzsch, attending to the words, correct a possible error of place: 'he came' means not into the sanctuary of the Lord — as v.20 proves to the contrary — but into his house or chamber, to pour out his heart before God and bend beneath his chastising hand; and the 'elders of his house' who tried to raise him were his oldest and most confidential servants. The witnesses agree in the end on a single portrait: a true penitent who neither presumes upon the promise nor rebels against it, but casts himself on the ground in the narrow hope that mercy may yet relent, and who reads even the fate of his child as a message about his standing with God. Prayer here is not the refusal of God's will but the submission of a broken man who dares, while there is breath in the child, to ask.
17:
The elders of the house rise to lift David from the ground, and he will neither rise nor eat; the witnesses read the refusal as the measure of his grief and its true source. The reformed Gill identifies the elders as 'some of the chief officers at court,' the men who had the management of the affairs of his household, come to persuade him to rise, sit, and take some food. The wesleyan Wesley agrees that they were 'the chief officers of his kingdom and household,' and then presses past the outward scene to the heart of it: David's excessive mourning 'did not proceed simply from the fear of the loss of the child; but from a deep sense of his sin, and the divine displeasure manifested herein.' It is the same reading Wesley gave at v.15 — the tears are less for the infant than for the God whose anger the infant's sickness declared.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch carry the narrative to its turn, joining this verse to the two that follow. On the seventh day, when the child died, David's servants were afraid to tell him, reasoning among themselves: 'Behold, while the child was still living, we spoke to him, and he did not hearken to our voice; how should we say to him, now the child is dead, that he should do harm?' — that is, do himself some injury in the depth of his anguish. The very intensity of David's grief while there was hope made his servants dread what it might become when hope was gone.

The witnesses do not divide, and the verse yields a single lesson about the shape of godly sorrow. David's immovable posture on the earth, his deafness to the appeals of his most trusted men, his refusal of bread — all belong, as Henry had said, to the evidence of a true repentance rather than to mere paternal distress. A grief that would not be comforted by rank or attendants or food is a grief reaching past the creature to the Creator; and the servants' fear, far from exaggerating it, testifies how deep it ran. The scene prepares the reader for the surprise of v.20, where the same man who would not be raised while the child lived will rise of his own accord the moment it dies — a composure that will puzzle his household precisely because they had watched the depth of his mourning here.
18:
The brief notice that 'on the seventh day the child died' occasions the witnesses' closest small dispute — from what point the seven days are counted. The reformed Gill argues that the seventh day is reckoned 'not the seventh day from its being taken ill, but from its birth; for it cannot be thought that David should fast seven days.' The wesleyan Wesley reads it the other way, taking the 'seventh day' as counted 'from the beginning of the distemper.' The two readings turn on a practical judgement about the length of David's fast: Gill will not have the king lie a full week without food, and so dates the seventh day from the child's birth, making the sickness itself the shorter span; Wesley, less troubled by that, lets the seven days run from the onset of the illness.

The greater part of the verse, however, concerns the servants' fear, and here the witnesses are agreed. As Keil and Delitzsch had explained over v.17, the men were afraid to tell David the child was dead, for they said one to another that a king who would not hearken to them while the child lived might, when told it was dead, 'do harm' — vex himself, in the depth of his anguish, even to his own hurt. Their silence is thus not negligence but a tender and anxious calculation, the fruit of having watched a grief they could not touch.

The verse teaches, in the witnesses' hands, how the very love of servants can misjudge a master whose sorrow they have measured only from the outside. They reason from themselves, expecting the death to redouble what the sickness had begun; they cannot foresee that David's grief was of a kind that death would still rather than inflame, because it was bound up with hope while hope remained and with submission once it was gone. The whispered conference the servants hold in the corner of the room is the very thing that will betray the news to David in v.19; and their mistaken dread sets in sharpest relief the calm that is about to astonish them — a calm that flows not from a shallow love but from a faith that can say, 'I shall go to him.'
19:
David perceives from his servants' whispering what they were afraid to speak, and the witnesses read the moment as the pivot of the whole episode. The reformed Gill notes the plain occasion of his discernment: the servants 'said the above to one another with a low voice, that he might not hear them, though in the same room with them,' and it was that hushed conference which told him all. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch describe the swift certainty of it: 'David saw at once what had happened from their whispering conversation, and asked whether the child was dead. When they answered in the affirmative,' he rose from the ground, washed and anointed himself, and changed his clothes — laying aside every sign of penitential grief and mourning — went into the house of the Lord and worshipped, then returned home and took food.

The witnesses do not divide, and the verse rewards a single, careful observation of David's bearing. He does not need to be told; he reads the truth in a murmur and a silence, and he meets it head-on with a direct question rather than fleeing the answer. There is nothing here of the frenzy his servants had feared. The man who could not be raised while the child lived rises unbidden the instant he learns it is dead, and the very promptness of his rising is the first sign that his grief had been governed all along by something other than helpless attachment.

What the interpreters draw out is the discipline of a faith that knows when to plead and when to submit. While the child breathed, prayer was in season, and David gave himself wholly to it; now that God has spoken his final word in the event itself, David bows to it without a murmur. The question 'Is the child dead?' is not the cry of a man breaking under the blow but of one bracing to accept it, and the actions that follow in v.20 — washing, worship, and bread — are the deliberate steps of a soul returning from the floor of penitence to the ordinary duties of life, having heard in the child's death the settled will of the God whose displeasure he had feared and whose mercy he now trusts.
20:
David rises, washes, anoints himself, changes his apparel, and goes first to worship and then to eat; the witnesses read each act as the deliberate liturgy of a chastened faith. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch mark the meaning of the change: in washing and anointing and putting on fresh clothing David 'laid aside all the signs of penitential grief and mourning,' and went into the house of the Lord — the holy tent upon Mount Zion — to worship, before returning to his own house to be fed. The wesleyan Clarke supplies the cultural background, noting that bathing, anointing the body, and changing the apparel are, among the peoples of the East, 'the first outward signs of coming out of a state of mourning or sickness.'

Wesley, also of the wesleyan school, presses the inward substance of the visit to the sanctuary. David 'came to the tabernacle to confess his sin before the Lord, to own his justice in this stroke, to deprecate his just displeasure, to acknowledge God's rich mercy in sparing his own life, and to offer such sacrifices as were required in such cases.' The worship is not resignation only but a whole act of penitent devotion — justice owned, mercy confessed, sacrifice brought — the man who had prayed for the child now bowing to the God who took it. The reformed Gill records simply that he arose 'from the floor on which he lay,' the plain hinge between the night of grief and the morning of submission.

The witnesses converge on the striking order of David's actions: worship comes before food. Before he will consult his own comfort he goes to the house of God, so that the first movement of the risen penitent is Godward. This is the very thing that will astonish his servants in v.21, for it reverses every expectation of nature, which looks for despair to deepen at the grave. Instead the witnesses find here the pattern of a faith that grieves without rebelling and submits without hardening — that can lay down the ashes of mourning, take up the ordinary garments of life, and carry its first breath back to God in worship. David's rising is not the forgetting of his sorrow but its consecration.
21:
The servants voice the astonishment the whole scene has been building toward — 'What thing is this that thou hast done? thou didst fast and weep for the child while it was alive; but when the child was dead, thou didst rise and eat bread' — and with only a single sound witness here the synthesis rests on the plain sense of their bewilderment. The reformed Gill catches the precise nature of their question: 'they knew what was done, but they did not know the meaning of it, which is what they inquired after.' They had watched every outward act; what escaped them was the logic that bound the mourning and the rising together.

The servants' perplexity is itself instructive, for it exposes how differently the world and a chastened faith reckon with grief. To the natural mind, mourning should deepen at death and slacken while hope survives; David has done the reverse, and so his conduct seems a riddle. Their measure of sorrow is the loss of the creature — the child gone, the comfort ended — and by that measure his composure is unaccountable. But David's sorrow, as Wesley had insisted over the preceding verses, was bound up with the sense of his sin and the displeasure of God; and a grief so grounded knows a season for pleading and a season for submission that mere affection cannot understand.

The verse therefore functions, in the witnesses' reading, as the question that David's great answer in vv.22-23 will resolve. The servants ask sincerely, not in reproach, and their honest confusion is the very occasion for one of the most moving confessions of faith in the Old Testament. By setting the puzzle plainly — fasting for the living, feasting after the dead — the narrative prepares the ground for David to unfold the reasoning of a believing heart: that while the child lived, who could tell whether God would be gracious, but now that it is dead, prayer has done its work, and there remains only the hope of a reunion beyond the grave. What looks to the household like inconsistency is in truth the coherence of a soul that has learned to bow.
22:
David explains the fasting the servants could not understand — 'while the child was yet alive, I fasted and wept: for I said, Who can tell whether God will be gracious to me, that the child may live?' — and the witnesses find in it the settled theology of prayer under the old covenant. The wesleyan Clarke states it as a principle: David, and indeed all under the Mosaic dispensation, 'were so satisfied that all God's threatenings and promises were conditional, that even in the most positive assertions relative to judgments they sought for a change of purpose.' Notwithstanding Nathan's positive word, David sought the child's life, 'not knowing but that might depend on some unexpressed condition, such as earnest prayer, fasting, humiliation'; and while there was hope he continued. When the child died he ceased to grieve, as he now saw such grief would be fruitless — and this, Clarke concludes, 'appears to be the sole reason of David's importunity.'

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch, rendering the words with Clericus, hold the same sense while guarding David's motive from the charge of unsubmissiveness. As von Gerlach's admirable remarks (which Ellicott too cites) had urged, in a man of so deep a penitence the prayer for the child sprang from another source than excessive love of a creature: David's 'Who can tell?' is not the presumption of one who thinks to bend God, but the humble uncertainty of one who dares to hope while the door is open, and who reads in the possible sparing of the child a possible token of restored favour.

The witnesses do not divide; they draw together into a small doctrine of importunate but resigned prayer. The believer under chastening does not treat even a declared judgment as closing every avenue of mercy, for he knows that God delights to be entreated and that many a threat carries an unspoken 'unless.' Yet the same faith that pleads while hope lasts also submits the instant God's will is made plain in the event. David's 'Who can tell whether God will be gracious' is the very language of a hope that is bold without being irreverent — a pattern, the interpreters imply, for every mourner who would pray hard and yet, when the answer is no, rise from the earth and worship. The reasoning that the servants asked for is here made plain, and its second half waits only for v.23.
23:
David completes his answer with words that reach beyond the grave — 'now he is dead, wherefore should I fast? can I bring him back again? I shall go to him, but he shall not return to me' — and here the witnesses openly divide over how much hope those words contain. The reformed Gill takes the first half at its plain force: to fast and pray now is 'to no purpose, no end can be thought to be answered by it,' for the child cannot be recalled.

On the famous clause 'I shall go to him,' the wesleyan Clarke is cautious. It is 'not clear,' he says, whether David here expressed his faith in the immortality of the soul; 'going to him may only mean, I also shall die, and be gathered to my fathers, as he is.' Yet Clarke will not leave the comfort in doubt, for 'whether David expressed this or not, we know that the thing is true,' and he calls the reunion of friends in a state of conscious existence 'one of the most solid grounds of consolation' to the bereaved — a hope so strong that even the heathen reached after it, as in the noble words he quotes from Cicero's Cato, longing for the day when he should join the divine company of souls gone before. The grammatical-historical Ellicott presses further and reads the clause as real faith. The words might, in themselves, be taken for a Stoic's cold comfort — 'I shall go to the dead, but the dead will not come to me' — but David 'in his whole nature and belief was as far as possible from being a Stoic,' and the phrase in his mouth 'can scarcely be anything else than an expression of confidence in a life of consciousness beyond the grave, and of the future recognition of those loved on earth.'

The debate, then, is fine but real: whether 'I shall go to him' asserts only that David too must die (Clarke's minimal reading) or affirms a conscious reunion with the child hereafter (Ellicott's fuller one). All the witnesses agree on the practical logic that ends the fast — the dead do not return to us, but we go to them — and Clarke closes with the note that lifts the whole scene into gospel light: 'we well know who has taught us not to sorrow as those without hope for departed friends.' Whatever the exact reach of David's knowledge, the comfort he gropes toward is the very comfort the New Testament will make sure, and his rising from the earth is the first fruit of a hope that death does not have the last word.
24:
Grief gives way to consolation and a new birth: 'David comforted Bathsheba his wife... and she bare a son, and he called his name Solomon: and the LORD loved him.' The reformed Gill notes that this is the first time she is called his wife, Uriah being dead and the marriage — displeasing to the Lord at first — now confirmed; and he pictures David comforting her sorrow by telling her that God had pardoned their iniquity and would give them another son to succeed him and build a house for his name. The wesleyan Wesley adds that Bathsheba 'was now much dejected, both for her former sin, and for the loss of the child,' and reads the closing clause as God's own word of grace: 'the Lord declared to David that he loved his son, notwithstanding the just cause David had given to God to alienate his affections from him.'

The grammatical-historical commentators dwell on the name and its timing. Keil and Delitzsch explain 'Solomon' as 'the man of peace' (1 Chron. 22:9), given because David 'regarded his birth as a pledge that he should now become a partaker again of peace with God' — not, they judge, from any reference to the Ammonite war being over, since Solomon was probably not born until after the capture of Rabbah. Clarke likewise takes the name prophetically, since Solomon 'signifies peaceable, and there was almost uninterrupted peace during his reign.' Ellicott presses the chronology and the theology together: the birth 'could hardly have taken place until after the events mentioned in vv.26-31,' and is set here, 'after the custom of Scripture narrative,' to close the story of Bathsheba in its proper connection — for the birth of that son who should succeed to the kingdom, 'and through whom should pass the line to the Messiah, was too important to be overlooked.'

The witnesses converge on the wonder that closes the chapter's darkest passage: 'the LORD loved him.' Out of the very union that had begun in adultery and blood, and after the stroke that had answered it, God brings a son of peace whom he names as beloved and sets in the royal and Messianic line. The interpreters do not divide; they read in Solomon's birth the sign that David's peace with God was truly restored, the pledge that grace can bring good even out of a mended breach, and the quiet opening of the way that runs from this cradle to the Son of David in whom all the promises are yes. The chastisement stands, but so does the covenant; and the last word over Bathsheba's second child is not judgement but love.
25:
A second name is given the child — 'he sent by the hand of Nathan the prophet; and he called his name Jedidiah, because of the LORD' — and the witnesses read it as a token of divine acceptance sealed through the very prophet who had rebuked the king. The reformed Gill canvasses the possible senses of 'he sent': that David sent word by Nathan to Bathsheba of the child's name, or delivered the boy into Nathan's hand to be educated and brought up in the nurture of the Lord, or — Gill's own preference — 'the Lord sent a message by Nathan the prophet to David, that he loved Solomon.' The name follows from that love: Jedidiah, as Wesley and Clarke both render it, means 'beloved of the LORD,' given, Wesley says, 'because of the Lord's love to him, or because the Lord commanded him to do so.'

The wesleyan and grammatical-historical commentators note what is unusual and what is passing in the naming. Clarke observes that this is 'the first instance I remember of a minister of God being employed to give a name to the child of one of his servants,' and finds it strange that, for all the honour of the prophet-given Jedidiah, 'the name given by the father' — Solomon — 'was that alone which prevailed.' Ellicott resolves the strangeness: the name Jedidiah 'was intended to do no more than express the Divine acceptance of Solomon,' and 'never came into use as a personal title.' It was a word of grace spoken over the cradle, not a name to be carried through life.

The witnesses do not divide, and the verse rewards the reader with a quiet reversal. The same Nathan who came bearing 'Thou art the man' now comes bearing 'beloved of the LORD'; the prophet of judgement becomes the herald of favour, and the child born after the breach is marked as the object of God's special love. That the endearing name should come by divine appointment, while the ordinary name stood in daily use, teaches that God's acceptance need not be worn as a badge to be real — it is enough that the Lord has spoken it. The chapter that opened with a sinner exposed closes with a son embraced, and Jedidiah stands as the pledge that where sin abounded, grace did much more abound, setting this beloved child in the line that leads to great David's greater Son.
26:
The narrative turns from the nursery to the battlefield, resuming the siege of Rabbah, and the witnesses read the transition for both its chronology and its moral. The grammatical-historical commentators settle the setting. Ellicott notes that the parallel account is resumed at 1 Chron. 20:2, and places Rabbah in the narrow valley of the upper Jabbok, its citadel on the northern cliff; the 'royal city' here and the 'city of waters' of the next verse mean the city proper, while the 'city' of vv.28-29 is the more strongly fortified citadel. Clarke resolves the apparent puzzle that Joab both 'took the royal city' and yet sent for David to come and take it: Rabbah was a city with a citadel, and Joab, having taken the king's residence, sends for David lest he take the whole and 'the city should be called after his name.' The reformed Gill adds that the finishing of the siege is set down here, out of its time, 'that the story concerning Bathsheba might lie together without any interruption.'

The puritan Matthew Henry draws the theology out of the victory. He marvels that God was so gracious as to give David this great success 'notwithstanding the sin he had been guilty of just at that time,' and the wicked use he had made of the sword of the Ammonites in the murder of Uriah. Justly might God have made that sword thenceforth a plague to David and his kingdom; instead 'he breaks it, and makes David's sword victorious, even before he repents, that this goodness of God might lead him to repentance' — good reason, Henry says, to own that God dealt not with him according to his sins (Ps. 103:10). He praises Joab too, who, having taken the city of waters, faithfully sent for his master to complete the action and have the praise of it, an example to the servants of the Lord Jesus to consult their Master's honour and not their own (Ps. 115:1).

The witnesses do not divide over the facts, but Henry alone presses on to a warning the verse anticipates. The same passage that displays God's undeserved kindness will display David 'too haughty and too severe' — too fond of the Ammonite crown in v.30 and too harsh with his prisoners in v.31. The success, in other words, is all of grace and none of merit: God magnifies his goodness in a man not yet made soft by repentance, that the victory itself might become another summons to the humility the chapter has been teaching. Even David's triumph is held up as a mercy meant to melt him.
27:
Joab's message to David — 'I have fought against Rabbah, and have taken the city of waters' — sets the witnesses to explaining what that watery epithet means and why it made haste necessary. The wesleyan Clarke offers two readings of the phrase. It may name 'the city where the tank or reservoir was that supplied the city and suburbs with water'; or the Hebrew may better be rendered 'I have intercepted, or cut off, the waters of the city' — a sense he supports from Houbigant and from Josephus, who writes that Joab, 'having cut off their waters,' brought the citadel to the brink of surrender. Either way, Clarke draws the practical point: this was why David must come speedily, for 'the citadel, deprived of water, could not long hold out.' Wesley more simply glosses that Rabbah was called the city 'of waters' 'because it was encompassed with water.'

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch attend to Joab's motive and the muster it required. The captain urges David to 'gather the rest of the people together, and encamp against the city, and take it: lest I take the city, and it be called after my name.' From David's needing to gather 'all the people' who had remained in the land, Keil infers that Joab's besieging army 'had been considerably weakened during the long siege, and at the capture of the water-city,' so that fresh force was genuinely wanted for the assault on the acropolis. The reformed Gill records only that Joab sent 'to acquaint him how he had proceeded, and what success he had had.'

The witnesses converge on the portrait of a loyal and self-denying servant, the counterpart to David's coming pride. Whatever the precise sense of the 'city of waters,' the point of Joab's message is his deliberate refusal to seize for himself the honour that belonged to his king. He had done the hard and costly work of the siege, yet he stands back at the decisive moment and summons David to strike the final blow and win the name. It is, as the interpreters observed over v.26, a fidelity that seeks the master's glory above the servant's — a small and bracing light of humility set just before the chapter records how little of that humility David himself would show in victory.
28:
Joab spells out his reason — 'gather the rest of the people together... and take it: lest I take the city, and it be called after my name' — and with the witnesses briefly gathered here the synthesis turns on his motive and his confidence. The grammatical-historical Ellicott weighs why the general should call for a fresh levy: Joab 'proposes a general muster of the remaining forces of Israel, either because additional force was actually needed for the capture of the citadel, or simply to carry out the formal capturing of the city by David in person.' The two possibilities are not opposed, and Ellicott leaves both open — the summons may answer a real military need or a point of royal honour, or both at once.

The wesleyan Wesley reads Joab's assurance and his deference together. 'For having taken one part of the city,' Wesley explains, 'he concluded the remaining part of it could not long stand out'; and the 'lest' that follows means 'lest I have the honour of taking it.' The reformed Gill adds only the plain sense of the command — to gather 'the rest of the soldiers in the land of Israel, and come to Rabbah.' All three keep the spotlight on the same trait: Joab, certain of victory, will not appropriate it.

The witnesses do not divide, and the verse teaches by Joab's example a lesson the chapter has been pressing on David. Here is a soldier who has borne the heat of a long siege, who could easily complete the conquest and let the captured capital bear his own name for ever, and who instead calls his king to finish what he began, that the glory may rest where it belongs. Whether the motive was necessity or courtesy or both, the effect is a studied self-effacement — the servant declining the crown of the achievement so that his lord may wear it. Set beside David's eagerness for the Ammonite king's literal crown in v.30, Joab's refusal of an honour within his reach reads almost as a rebuke, and reminds every reader that true greatness is often shown most in the honours one is willing to forgo.
29:
The verse is a single stroke of narrative — 'David gathered all the people together, and went to Rabbah, and fought against it, and took it' — and with only the reformed Gill speaking here the synthesis draws its substance from his brief note and from the verse's own words. Gill explains the double purpose of the general muster: David gathered the soldiers that were with him or near him 'partly to recruit Joab's troops, who, by the continuance of the siege, and the sallies of the enemy on them, might be greatly diminished; and partly to make conquests of other cities of the Ammonites, and to carry off the spoil of them.' The gathering answers, that is, both to the weakened state of the besieging army — the very inference Keil had drawn at v.27 — and to the wider campaign the fall of the capital would open.

Read against the preceding verses, the movement completes the picture of loyal order between king and captain. Joab had summoned, and David comes; the general's self-denial is met by the king's prompt obedience to the call, so that the honour of the conquest falls, as Joab intended, upon the crown. The verse quietly vindicates the arrangement of v.28: the fresh people are gathered, the acropolis is stormed, and the city that had defied a long siege is taken at last with David present to receive its surrender.

The interpreters find in the terse report the ordinary shape of a decisive victory and the seam of the story's structure. This capture is the hinge on which the closing paragraph turns, for it makes possible both the taking of the king's crown in v.30 and the reduction of the Ammonite cities in v.31. The chapter that has moved from a private sin exposed to a public war concluded now brings David to the height of his outward success — the very success that, as Henry warned, will test the temper of a man not yet fully humbled, and expose in triumph the pride and severity that repentance had not yet cured. The taking of Rabbah is thus both an ending and a threshold, closing the siege and opening the troubling scene of David's conduct in his hour of conquest.
30:
David takes the Ammonite crown — 'a talent of gold with the precious stones' — and sets it on his own head, and the witnesses divide over two words: whose crown it was, and what a 'talent' here measures. The reformed Gill reads it plainly as the crown of Hanun, king of the Ammonites, stripped of his ensigns of royalty by the David he had so shamefully insulted. The grammatical-historical Ellicott notes a curiosity of the text: the same Hebrew letters rendered 'their king' also form 'Milcom,' the chief idol of the Ammonites, so that some have supposed the idol's crown is meant — a conjecture he judges 'quite unnecessary.' Keil and Delitzsch keep to the king, observing that its being taken 'from off his head' implies the Ammonite monarch 'had either been taken prisoner or slain at the capture of the city.'

The weight is the sharper debate. Keil takes the talent as a true weight — some eighty-three pounds — but concludes that since the strongest man could hardly bear such a crown, the figure rests 'not upon actual weighing, but simply upon an approximative estimate, which is somewhat too high.' The wesleyan voices prefer to read value rather than mass. Clarke urges that if 'the weight whereof' be taken as the worth, all is plain — the gold and its jewels together being equal in value to a talent of gold, about five thousand pounds sterling — which suits the added phrase 'with the precious stones,' since stones are not reckoned by the weight of gold. Wesley agrees: the word means 'rather the price,' for the addition of precious stones 'makes this signification most probable,' and the weight 'might seem too great' for either the Ammonite king or David to wear. Ellicott offers a third path, allowing the talent as weight but a lighter one — a Babylonian, Persian, or Ammonite talent smaller than the Hebrew — while granting that on any reasonable supposition the crown 'would have been too great to allow of being commonly worn.'

Beneath the arithmetic the witnesses read the same sober meaning, one Henry had already pressed. The crown was of extraordinary value, and David 'would have it set upon his head, though it would have been better to have cast it at God's feet, and at this time to have put his own mouth in the dust, under guilt.' The heart truly humbled for sin, Henry says, is dead to worldly glory and looks on it with a holy contempt. Whether the talent names weight or worth, whether the crown was Hanun's or Milcom's, the interpreters agree that a man so lately broken over his sin now reaches, perhaps too eagerly, for a conqueror's splendour — and that the great spoil brought out of the city sets in relief how far outward triumph may run ahead of inward humbling.
31:
The chapter ends on its most disputed verse — David 'brought forth the people... and put them under saws, and under harrows of iron... and made them pass through the brickkiln' — and the great division among the witnesses is whether this describes torture and execution or forced labour. The wesleyan Clarke leads the milder reading. A great cry, he says, has been raised against 'David's unparalleled, if not diabolic, cruelty,' drawn chiefly from the parallel 1 Chron. 20:3, 'he cut them with saws.' But the word here, he argues, differs by a single letter from that in Chronicles, and several manuscripts even of Chronicles read it as our verse does; the sense is that David 'made the people slaves, and employed them in sawing, making iron harrows, or mining... and in hewing of wood, and making of brick.' 'Sawing asunder, hacking, chopping, and hewing human beings,' Clarke insists, 'have no place in this text,' and he laments how much advantage unbelievers have gained from an inaccurate translation.

Against this stands the weight of the grammatical-historical and wesleyan witnesses who read a real execution. Keil and Delitzsch grant that the received 'he put them into the saw' yields no sense, and by the same one-letter emendation read 'he cut (sawed) them in pieces' with iron cutting-tools — an execution 'with cruel tortures.' Wesley too takes it that David 'sawed them to death,' of which punishment there are examples in Scripture and other authors, and reads the brickkiln as making them 'pass through the furnace of Malchen, that is, of Moloch,' punishing them with their own sin. Ellicott follows the emendation toward 1 Chron. 20:3 and Heb. 11:37, takes the harrows as heavy iron threshing-tools, and notes the Hebrew margin 'through Malchan,' whence some suppose David made the Ammonites pass through the fire by which they had consecrated their own children to Molech. Yet Ellicott adds the historical charity that in these cruelties 'David acted in accordance with the customs and the knowledge of his time' — abhorrent to the spirit of Christianity, but taken by David and his contemporaries 'as matters of course.'

The reformed Gill and the puritan Matthew Henry narrow and moralize the scene. Gill holds that David brought forth not all the inhabitants but 'the princes of the children of Ammon, the counsellors of Hanun,' who had advised the shameful usage of David's ambassadors; and Wesley likewise restrains it to the men of war and chiefly those authors of the outrage. Henry judges the act too severe whichever way it is read: to kill men 'in cold blood, and by cruel tortures' did not become the king who had promised to sing of mercy as well as judgment, and was 'a sign that David's heart was not yet made soft by repentance.' So the witnesses close the chapter as they will not fully agree — divided over saws and slavery, over Molech's fire and a brick-kiln's toil — yet united in the lesson Henry draws: that we are most compassionate and forgiving to others when we most feel our own need of the Lord's forgiving love; and that even a pardoned man, before repentance has done its softening work, may act with a rigour his better self would abhor.
